reasoning:
The Artemis Accords have sustained a strong momentum of accessions since 2023, with approximately 11 of the last 17 months seeing at least one new signatory (a ~65% base rate). Over the past six months alone, multiple new nations have joined, including Bangladesh in April 2025 and Norway in May 2025. 

Furthermore, the month of June provides excellent venues for space diplomacy that tend to cluster such announcements. The UN Committee on the Peaceful Uses of Outer Space (COPUOS) holds its 68th session in Vienna starting June 25, 2025. Historically, the start of COPUOS corresponds with major diplomatic pronouncements. In addition, the Paris Air Show takes place in mid-June 2025, which is another frequent anchor for aerospace and space agreements. Although many of the primary U.S. space allies have already signed (leaving slightly less "low-hanging fruit" among the remaining nations), discussions are known to be active with others, such as Portugal, which formally signaled intent a year prior. 

The primary risks for a NO resolution are random Poisson variance (simply no country finalizing their paperwork in time for June), or a COPUOS signing being delayed to the July 1-4 portion of the conference schedule, missing the June 30 deadline. Adjusting the strong ~65% base rate upward to account for the major diplomatic events, a 74% probability optimally straddles the robust historical trend and the event-driven catalysts without assuming absolute certainty.
